source_label: Mastering Lightning Network — Chapter 8: Routing on a Network of Payment Channels
excerpt: The HTLC chain enforces atomicity end-to-end: the preimage propagates back from the receiver to the sender as each hop claims its own incoming HTLC. Either every hop settles or every hop times out. There is no in-between state where some intermediary keeps funds without forwarding them — the cryptography makes that arithmetically impossible.
body:
Source excerpt:
The HTLC chain enforces atomicity end-to-end: the preimage propagates back from the receiver to the sender as each hop claims its own incoming HTLC. Either every hop settles or every hop times out. There is no in-between state where some intermediary keeps funds without forwarding them — the cryptography makes that arithmetically impossible.

---

Cited by leaf "HTLCs, Sphinx onion routing, MPP":
A *Hash Time-Locked Contract* (HTLC) is the off-chain primitive that lets a Lightning payment hop across multiple channels without any single intermediary being able to steal the funds in flight. The receiver of a payment generates a random 32-byte preimage, hashes it (SHA-256), and embeds the hash in an invoice. The sender constructs a route through the channel graph and adds an HTLC to its first-hop channel that says, in effect: *pay these sats to anyone who can produce a preimage hashing to this hash, OR refund to me after timeout T* [1].

Each hop along the route does the same on its outgoing channel, with a slightly shorter timeout (the `cltv_delta`), forwarding a new HTLC offer with the same payment hash. When the chain of HTLCs reaches the receiver, they reveal the preimage to settle the last HTLC, which propagates the preimage back up the chain — each intermediary needs the preimage to claim its own incoming HTLC, so they have a strict economic incentive to forward it. The atomicity property follows: either every hop on the route settles, or every hop times out and refunds. Partial settlement is impossible [1].

The route itself is encoded as a *Sphinx onion*. Each hop only sees the previous hop and the next hop; intermediaries cannot tell how long the route is or where the payment originated or terminates. This is structurally identical to Tor's onion routing, adapted for the bounded geometry of a payment graph [2]. The sender constructs the onion locally from the channel graph it has gossiped from peers, picks a path, encrypts each layer with the corresponding hop's public key, and ships it along with the first HTLC.

*Multi-Path Payments* (MPP, BOLT-4 extension) split a single logical payment across multiple routes that share the same payment hash. The receiver waits to see the full amount accumulate across all parts before revealing the preimage — atomicity is preserved across the bundle. This solved the practical capacity problem where no single channel had enough liquidity in the right direction to route a large payment by itself [3]. *AMP* (Atomic Multi-Path) extends MPP further by deriving per-part payment hashes from a master secret, which removes the requirement that the sender know the receiver's specific invoice in advance.

Routing on Lightning is necessarily probabilistic. The sender doesn't know which channels along its candidate path have enough liquidity in the right direction at the moment of the attempt; failures are absorbed by retrying with a different route. This is inherent to a network where channel state is private — if every node had to publish its current balance, Lightning would lose most of its privacy properties. Modern path-finding algorithms like Pickhardt-Richter min-cost flow treat liquidity as a probability distribution and minimise *expected* total cost across attempts rather than picking a single nominally-cheapest path.
